Control_Clause: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently based on valid legal grounds including consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public interest, or legitimate interests.
Personal data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes, and not further processed in a manner incompatible with those purposes.
Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure and demonstrate compliance with PDPL, including staff training, access controls, and security measures.
Data subjects have the right to access their personal data and obtain information about processing activities including purposes retention periods and recipients.
Data subjects have the right to rectification of inaccurate personal data and completion of incomplete data.
Data subjects have the right to erasure (right to be forgotten) under specific circumstances including unlawful processing, purpose fulfillment, or consent withdrawal.
Data subjects have the right to restrict processing under specific circumstances including accuracy disputes or legitimate interest objections.
Data subjects have the right to data portability to receive and transmit personal data in a structured commonly used and machine-readable format.
Data subjects have the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests or for direct marketing purposes including automated decision-making.

Controllers and processors must implement appropriate security measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access, disclosure, alteration, and destruction, including encryption pseudonymization and access controls.
Controllers must notify SDAIA of personal data breaches within 72 hours and affected data subjects without undue delay when the breach poses high risk to rights and freedoms.
Controllers must maintain Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) documenting processing purposes, data categories, recipients, retention periods, and security measures.
Controllers and processors must designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO) when processing large-scale sensitive data, systematic monitoring, or public authority processing.
Controllers must conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for high-risk processing including new technologies large-scale systematic monitoring or sensitive data.
Personal data may only be transferred outside Saudi Arabia if the destination has adequate protection, appropriate safeguards are in place, or with explicit consent or legal derogations.
Controllers must ensure processors provide sufficient guarantees of compliance, use written contracts specifying processing instructions, security measures, and confidentiality obligations.
Processing children's personal data requires verifiable parental consent and additional protections appropriate to the child's age and understanding.

Processing sensitive personal data (health, biometric, genetic, racial, ethnic, political, religious, philosophical beliefs, trade union, sexual orientation, criminal records) requires explicit consent or specific legal grounds and enhanced safeguards.
